# Episode 6: Creating Your Own Container Images {.nonincremental}

::: {.callout-tip icon="false" .nonincremental}
## Questions

- How can I make my own Docker container images?
- How do I document the 'recipe' for a Docker container image?
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false" .nonincremental}
## Objectives

- "Explain the purpose of a `Dockerfile` and show some simple examples."
- "Demonstrate how to build a Docker container image from a `Dockerfile`."
- Compare the steps of creating a container image interactively versus a `Dockerfile`.
- Create an installation strategy for a container image.
- "Demonstrate how to upload ('push') your container images to the Docker Hub."
- Describe the significance of the Docker Hub naming scheme.
:::

---

## Why Create Your Own Container Images

There are lots of reasons why you might want to create your **own** Docker container image.

- You can't find a container image with all the tools you need on Docker Hub.
- You want to have a container image to "archive" all the specific software versions you ran for a project.
- You want to share your workflow with someone else.

## Interactive Installation

Before building a reproducible image, test installation steps interactively.

- Start an interactive container from `alpine`:

```bash
docker container run -it alpine sh
```

`alpine` is minimal, so tools like `python3` are not preinstalled.

```bash
/# python3
```

```output
sh: python3: not found
```

Install Python using Alpine's package manager (`apk`):

```bash
/# apk add --update python3 py3-pip python3-dev
```

Verify the install:

```bash
/# python3 --version
```


---

## Challenge

::: {.callout-note .challenge-callout icon="false" }

## Searching for Help

Can you find instructions for installing R on Alpine Linux? Do they work?

:::


---

## Installing R on Alpine

::: {.callout-note .solution-callout icon="false"  collapse="true"}

## Solution

A quick search should hopefully show that the way to install R on Alpine Linux is:

```bash
/# apk add R
```

:::

When you exit, these interactive changes are not captured as a reproducible image.

- There are commands that will "snapshot" the changes. Snapshot-style workflows are possible, but hard to reproduce.
- Better approach: encode steps in a `Dockerfile` to create a container image.

If you haven't already, exit out of the interactively running container.

```bash
/# exit
```

## Put Installation Instructions in a `Dockerfile`

A `Dockerfile` is a plain-text recipe for building container images.

Inspect the starter `Dockerfile`:

```bash
cd ~/Desktop/docker-intro/basic
cat Dockerfile
```

```output
FROM <EXISTING IMAGE>
RUN <INSTALL CMDS FROM SHELL>
CMD <CMD TO RUN BY DEFAULT>
```

What each instruction means:

- `FROM`: which container image we’re starting with. It is the "base" container image we are going to start from.
- `RUN`: installation commands we want to run. These are the same commands that we used interactively above.
- `CMD`: the default command we want a container based on this container image to run, if no other command is provided. 
  
  - It is recommended
  to provide `CMD` in *exec-form* (see the
  [`CMD` section](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/builder/#cmd)
  of the Dockerfile documentation for more details).
  - It is written as a
  list which contains the executable to run as its first element,
  optionally followed by any arguments as subsequent elements. 
  - The list
  is enclosed in square brackets (`[]`) and its elements are
  double-quoted (`"`) strings which are separated by commas. 
  - For
  example, `CMD ["ls", "-lF", "--color", "/etc"]` would translate
  to `ls -lF --color /etc`.


---

::: {.callout-note}

## *Shell-Form* and *Exec-Form* for CMD

Another way to specify the parameter for the
[`CMD` instruction](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/builder/#cmd)
is the *shell-form*. Here you type the command as you would call it
from the command line. Docker then silently runs this command in the
image's standard shell.  `CMD cat /etc/passwd` is equivalent to `CMD ["/bin/sh", "-c", "cat /etc/passwd"]`. We recommend to prefer the
more explicit *exec-form* because we will be able to create more
flexible container image command options and make sure complex commands
are unambiguous in this format.


:::


---

## Challenge

::: {.callout-note .challenge-callout icon="false" }

## Exercise: Take a Guess

Do you have any ideas about what we should use to fill in the sample Dockerfile
to replicate the installation we did above?

:::


---

## Solution

::: {.callout-note .solution-callout icon="false"  collapse="true"}

## Solution:

Based on our experience above, edit the `Dockerfile` (in your text editor of choice)
to look like this:

```dockerfile
FROM alpine
RUN apk add --update python3 py3-pip python3-dev
CMD ["python3", "--version"]
```

:::


This Dockerfile does three things:

- starts from Alpine Linux;
- installs Python 3, pip package management tool, and Python header files;
- sets default runtime command to print the Python 3 version.

## Create a New Docker Image

At this point, you only have a `Dockerfile`.
Use `docker image build` to turn it into an image.

`docker image build` needs two key inputs:

- Dockerfile location (build context)
- output image name (typically `USERNAME/CONTAINER_IMAGE_NAME`)

Typical command format:

```bash
docker image build -t USERNAME/CONTAINER_IMAGE_NAME .
```

`-t` sets the output image name.
Final `.` means: use current directory as build context.

Example:

```bash
docker image build -t alice/alpine-python .
```


---

## Build Context

::: {.callout-note}

## Build Context

- The final argument to `docker image build` is a directory, not the Dockerfile itself.
- That directory is the **build context**.
- Docker expects the Dockerfile and any copied files to be available there.
- Keep the build context small so Docker only loads what you actually need.


:::


---

## Challenge

::: {.callout-note .challenge-callout icon="false" }

## Exercise: Review!

1. Think back to earlier. What command can you run to check if your container image was created
  successfully? (Hint: what command shows the container images on your computer?)

2. We didn't specify a tag for our container image name. What tag did Docker automatically use?

3. What command will run a container based on the container image you've created? What should happen by default
  if you run such a container? Can you make it do something different, like print
  "hello world"?

:::


---

## Solution

::: {.callout-note .solution-callout icon="false"  collapse="true"}

## Solution

1. To see your new image, run `docker image ls`. You should see the name of your new
  container image under the "REPOSITORY" heading.

2. In the output of `docker image ls`, you can see that Docker has automatically
  used the `latest` tag for our new container image.

3. We want to use `docker container run` to run a container based on a container image.

The following command should run a container and print out our default message, the version
of Python:

```bash
docker container run alice/alpine-python
```

To run a container based on our container image and print out "Hello world" instead:

```bash
docker container run alice/alpine-python echo "Hello World"
```

:::

This is a major milestone:

- you combined a Linux base image with your own setup;
- the same container can run consistently across macOS, Windows, Linux, and cloud systems.

## Boring but Important Notes About Installation

Installation strategy matters. Use these guidelines:

- **Start from the right base image**:
  use official Python/R images where possible.
- **Keep images lean**:
  install only what you need.
- **Use distro-appropriate package tools**:
  Alpine `apk`, Ubuntu `apt/apt-get`, CentOS `yum`, etc.
- **Reuse familiar installers**:
  tools like `pip` or `install.packages()` can still be useful inside containers (if the basic scripting language is installed).
- **Follow upstream docs**:
  README/install guides often provide the best Linux setup path.

A practical installation workflow:

- Make a list of what you want to install.
- Look for pre-existing container images.
- Read through instructions for software you'll need to install.
- Try installing everything interactively in your base container -- take notes!
- From your interactive installation, create a `Dockerfile` and then try to build
  the container image from that.

## Share Your New Container Image on Docker Hub

To publish publicly, push your image to Docker Hub.

- If the name follows the Docker Hub pattern, use `docker image push`:

```bash
docker image push alice/alpine-python
```

Use your full image name.

After pushing, confirm on [https://hub.docker.com](https://hub.docker.com) that the image appears in your account.



::: {.callout-note}

## Logging In

- You must be logged in before pushing to Docker Hub.
- If `docker image push` fails, run `docker login` first.
- Then retry the push command.


:::

## What's in a Name? (Again)

Locally, you can name images however you like.
For sharing, use Docker Hub naming format:

- `USERNAME/CONTAINER_IMAGE_NAME:TAG`

For example, imagine someone
named Alice has been working on a workflow container image and called it `workflow-test`
on her own computer. She now wants to share it in her `alice` Docker Hub account
with the name `workflow-complete` and a tag of `v1`.

Use `docker image tag` to rename/re-tag images for publishing.

Example:

```bash
docker image tag workflow-test alice/workflow-complete:v1
```

Then push the tagged image:

- `docker image push alice/workflow-complete:v1`

---

## Automate Image Publishing with GitHub Actions

GitHub Actions can build a Docker image and publish it to Docker Hub automatically whenever you push to your repository.

Typical workflow:

1. Create or clone a GitHub repository
2. Add a `Dockerfile`
3. Add Docker Hub credentials as GitHub secrets
4. Add a workflow file
5. Push to `main` to trigger the build

---

## Step 1: Create or Clone a Repository

Clone an existing repository:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/Sanduni-P/publish-docker-images.git
cd publish-docker-images
```

Or create a new repository locally:

```bash
mkdir publish-docker-images
cd publish-docker-images
git init
```

If you create a new repository locally, also create an empty repository on GitHub.
You will push your files there later, and that is where you will add the Actions secrets.

---

## Step 2: Add a `Dockerfile`

Add a `Dockerfile` in the repository root.

```dockerfile
FROM alpine
RUN apk add --update python3 py3-pip python3-dev
CMD ["python3", "--version"]
```

This is the image definition that GitHub Actions will build and publish.

---

## Step 3: Create a Docker Hub Access Token

First, log in to Docker Hub in your browser.

Then create a personal access token:

- open your profile settings;
- go to Personal access tokens;
- create a new token such as `github-actions`;
- give it Read and Write permissions;
- copy the token and store it safely.

We use a token instead of a password so the workflow can authenticate securely.

---

## Step 4: Add GitHub Repository Secrets

In your GitHub repository:

- open **Settings**;
- go to **Secrets and variables** -> **Actions** -> **New repository secret**;
- add `DOCKERHUB_USERNAME`;
- add `DOCKERHUB_TOKEN`.

These secrets are available to the workflow at runtime and keep credentials out of the repository.

---

## Step 5: Add the Workflow File

Create `.github/workflows/docker-publish.yml`.

This file is written in YAML.

- `name` gives the workflow a label in GitHub;
- `on` says when the workflow should run;
- `jobs` defines the work to perform;
- `steps` lists the actions executed in order.

---

## Workflow Example

```yaml
name: Publish Docker image

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

jobs:
  push_to_registry:
    name: Push Docker image to Docker Hub
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      packages: write
      contents: read
      attestations: write
      id-token: write
    steps:
      - name: Check out the repo
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Log in to Docker Hub
        uses: docker/login-action@f4ef78c080cd8ba55a85445d5b36e214a81df20a
        with:
          username: ${{ secrets.DOCKERHUB_USERNAME }}
          password: ${{ secrets.DOCKERHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Extract metadata (tags, labels) for Docker
        id: meta
        uses: docker/metadata-action@9ec57ed1fcdbf14dcef7dfbe97b2010124a938b7
        with:
          images: your-dockerhub-username/your-image-name
          tags: |
            type=raw,value=latest
            type=sha

      - name: Build and push Docker image
        id: push
        uses: docker/build-push-action@3b5e8027fcad23fda98b2e3ac259d8d67585f671
        with:
          context: .
          file: ./Dockerfile
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
          labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }}

      - name: Generate artifact attestation
        uses: actions/attest@v4
        with:
          subject-name: index.docker.io/your-dockerhub-username/your-image-name
          subject-digest: ${{ steps.push.outputs.digest }}
          push-to-registry: true
```

---

## What This Workflow Does

- Starts a fresh Ubuntu runner on GitHub.
- Checks out your repository.
- Logs in to Docker Hub using the stored secrets.
- Generates tags and labels for the image.
- Builds the image from your `Dockerfile`.
- Pushes the image to Docker Hub.
- Creates an attestation recording where the image came from.

Update `your-dockerhub-username` and `your-image-name` before using it.

---

## Step 6: Commit and Push

If you cloned an existing repository:

```bash
git add .
git commit -m "Add Dockerfile and GitHub Actions workflow"
git push origin main
```

If you created a new local repository:

```bash
git add .
git commit -m "Add Dockerfile and GitHub Actions workflow"
git branch -M main
git remote add origin <your-github-repository-url>
git push -u origin main
```

Pushing to `main` triggers the workflow.

---

## Step 7: Check the Workflow Run

On GitHub:

- open the repository;
- go to the **Actions** tab;
- open the latest workflow run;
- confirm that the build and push steps completed successfully.

If it succeeds, your image should now be available on Docker Hub.

---

## Challenge

::: {.callout-note .challenge-callout icon="false" }

## Run Image Created from GitHub Actions

Can you run the container image that was just published?

:::

---

## Solution

::: {.callout-note .solution-callout icon="false" collapse="true"}

## Solution

Replace `your-dockerhub-username/your-image-name` with your Docker Hub ysername and the image name you just used:

```bash
docker container run your-dockerhub-username/your-image-name
```

:::
